body: What is the main role of Ctf4 in dna replication?
gold_exact_answer: [['Coordination of the progression of helicase and DNA polymerase alpha at the eukaryotic replication fork.', '']]
gold_ideal_answer: ['Ctf4 coordinates the progression of helicase and DNA polymerase alpha.  Mcm10 and And-1/CTF4 recruit DNA polymerase alpha to chromatin for initiation of DNA replication. And-1/Ctf4 is therefore a new replication initiation factor that brings together the MCM2-7 helicase and the DNA pol alpha-primase complex, analogous to the linker between helicase and primase or helicase and polymerase that is seen in the bacterial replication machinery.', 'coupling MCM2-7 to replicative polymerases is an important feature of the regulation of chromosome replication in eukaryotes, and highlight a key role for Ctf4 in this processAnd-1/Ctf4 is therefore a new replication initiation factor that brings together the MCM2-7 helicase and the DNA pol alpha-primase complex, analogous to the linker between helicase and primase or helicase and polymerase that is seen in the bacterial replication machinery', 'coupling MCM2-7 to replicative polymerases is an important feature of the regulation of chromosome replication in eukaryotes, and highlight a key role for Ctf4 in this processAnd-1/Ctf4 is therefore a new replication initiation factor that brings together the MCM2-7 helicase and the DNA pol alpha-primase complex, analogous to the linker between helicase and primase or helicase and polymerase that is seen in the bacterial replication machinery']
pred_ideal_answer: Ctf4 plays a central role in DNA replication. In Drosophila, Ctf4 is essential for efficient DNA replication and normal cell cycle progression. In budding yeast, Ctf4p facilitates Mcm10p to promote DNA replication. Furthermore, in humans, the Ctf4/AND-1 factor influences DNA replication. Ctf4 coordinates the progression of helicase and DNA polymerase alpha, and in conjunction with Ctf18, it coordinates the relative movement of the replication fork with respect to the cohesin ring.

body: What is the clinical indication of cardiac T1 mapping magnetic resonance?
gold_exact_answer: [['T1 mapping can quantitatively characterize myocardial tissue, i.e. fibrosis and edema.']]
gold_ideal_answer: ['T1 mapping can quantitatively characterize myocardial tissue, in particular diffuse and interstitial fibrosis, edema in both overt and subclinical cardiophyopathies. However more research is required before a large-scale application for clinical decision-making can be recommended.']
pred_ideal_answer: Cardiac magnetic resonance T1 mapping is utilized for myocardial characterization and quantification. Several studies demonstrate its application in assessing myocardial tissue properties, including identifying areas of fibrosis, assessing myocardial viability, and evaluating the effects of various cardiac conditions. T1 mapping is frequently employed in the context of acute myocardial infarction, characterizing myocardial edema and assessing infarct size. Furthermore, it has applications in evaluating left atrial myocardium, and evaluating fibrosis in the left ventricle.

body: Which hormone abnormalities are characteristic to Pendred syndrome?
gold_ideal_answer: ['Thyroid hormone abnormalities are characteristic to Pendred syndrome. Hypothyroidism is the most common thyroid hormone abnormality in Pendred syndrome. Pendred syndrome  is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by sensorineural deafness, goiter and a partial defect in iodide organification.']
pred_ideal_answer: Pendred syndrome is characterized by hormone abnormalities related to iodide transport in the thyroid. Studies have shown defects in thyroidal iodide transport and abnormalities in thyroid hormone levels in patients with this syndrome. The genetics of Pendred syndrome is associated with mutations in the PRM1 gene, which encodes a protein involved in iodide transport. Clinical features of the syndrome include hypothyroidism, goiter, and hearing loss.

body: Which is the main calcium pump of the sarcoplasmic reticulum?
gold_ideal_answer: ['Sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca(2+)-ATPase (SERCA) is the pump crucial for calcium homeostasis. SERCA is a membrane protein that belongs to the family of P-type ion translocating ATPases and pumps free cytosolic calcium into intracellular stores.']
pred_ideal_answer: The SERCA pump is the main calcium pump of the sarcoplasmic reticulum. Several studies highlight the SERCA pump's role in regulating calcium cycling within the sarcoplasmic reticulum, a critical function for muscle contraction and cellular signaling. The histidine-rich calcium binding protein has also been identified as a regulator of sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium cycling, further emphasizing the importance of these pumps in maintaining calcium homeostasis.

body: What is the characteristic feature of the Dyke-Davidoff-Masson syndrome.

Calvarial thickening, skull and facial asymmetry, contralateral hemiparesis, cognitive impairment and seizures are also characteristic to the Dyke-Davidoff-Masson syndrome.\n.']
pred_ideal_answer: The characteristic feature of Dyke-Davidoff-Masson syndrome is periorbital livedo reticularis. This is a livedo reticularis that involves the eyelids and surrounding areas. body: Which protein is the E3-ubiquitin ligase that targets the tumor suppressor p53 for proteasomal degradation?

gold_ideal_answer: ['The p53 tumour suppressor protein is tightly controlled by the E3 ubiquitin ligase, mouse double minute 2 (MDM2). The RING domain E3 ubiquitin ligase Mdm2 is the master regulator of the tumor suppressor p53. pred_ideal_answer: The RING domain E3 ubiquitin ligase Mdm2 is the master regulator of the tumor suppressor p53. It targets p53 for proteasomal degradation, restraining the potent activity of p53 and enabling cell survival and proliferation.

Aside from its ubiquitin ligase function, Mdm2 has been believed to be capable of suppressing p53's transcriptional activity by binding with and masking the transactivation domain of p53. The ability of Mdm2 to restrain p53 activity by binding alone, without ubiquitination, was challenged by a 2007 study using a", 'Abstract: The RING domain E3 ubiquitin ligase Mdm2 is the master regulator of the tumor suppressor p53. body: What is the enzymatic activity of the breast cancer associated gene BRCA1?
gold_ideal_answer: ['Discovering the precise function of the breast and ovarian specific tumor suppressor, BRCA1, has proven to be quite complicated. The protein encoded by BRCA1 interacts in vivo with the related BARD1 protein to form a heterodimeric complex that acts as a ubiquitin E3 ligase. E3-ubiquitin ligase activity is the only known enzymatic activity of BRCA1, which is mediated by the N-terminal RING finger domain.', 'E3-ubiquitin ligase activity is the only known enzymatic activity of BRCA1, which is mediated by the N-terminal RING finger domain.BRCA1 nuclear transport and ubiquitin E3 ligase enzymatic activity are tightly regulated by the BRCA1 dimeric binding partner BARD1 and further modulated by cancer mutations and diverse signaling pathways.', 'E3-ubiquitin ligase activity is the only known enzymatic activity of BRCA1, which is mediated by the N-terminal RING finger domain.BRCA1 nuclear transport and ubiquitin E3 ligase enzymatic activity are tightly regulated by the BRCA1 dimeric binding partner BARD1 and further modulated by cancer mutations and diverse signaling pathways.']
pred_ideal_answer: BRCA1 tumor suppression depends on BRCT phosphoprotein binding, but not its E3 ligase activity.

body: Which fusion protein is involved in the development of Ewing sarcoma?
gold_ideal_answer: ['Ewing sarcoma is the second most common bone malignancy in children and young adults. body: What is the effect of the absence of Saccharomyces cerevisiae Rrm3p?

body: Which intermediate filament (IF) protein can be used as a non-specific marker of the neuronal precursor cells of the subventricular zone?
gold_ideal_answer: ["Nestin can be used as a nonspecific marker protein for precursor cells in the subventricular zone (SVZ). Nestin is a unique intermediate filament protein. While it is robustly expressed in developing brain, postnatal expression is limited to the brain's SVZ."]
pred_ideal_answer: Nestin is an intermediate filament protein expressed in neuroepithelial stem cells during development and it is later replaced by cell specific neuronal or glial filaments. Nevertheless, nestin⁺ cells remain within adult tissues and they can be regarded as potential neural stem cell (NSC).

body: Which is the most typical peptide sequence responsible for retrieval of endoplasmic reticulum (ER) lumenal proteins from the Golgi apparatus?
gold_ideal_answer: ['The lumenal endoplasmic reticulum (ER) proteins carry a specific sorting signal which enables their retrieval from multiple post-ER compartments (up to the TGN along the exocytotic pathway), back to the ER. The most typical such signal is the carboxyl-terminal Lys-Asp-Glu-Leu (KDEL), which is bound by a KDEL receptor in the Golgi apparatus, as well as in the intermediate compartment. Thus KDEL functions as a retrieval signal of lumenal ER proteins from Golgi to ER.']

LepChorionDB is freely available at http://bioinformatics.biol.uoa.gr/LepChorionDB.', 'Abstract: Chorion proteins of Lepidoptera have a tripartite structure, which consists of a central domain and two, more variable, flanking arms. The central domain is highly conserved and it is used for the classification of chorion proteins into two major classes, A and B. Annotated and unreviewed Lepidopteran chorion protein sequences are available in various databases. A database, named LepChorionDB, was constructed by searching 5 different protein databases using class A and B central', 'was constructed by searching 5 different protein databases using class A and B central domain-specific profile Hidden Markov Models (pHMMs), developed in this work. body: Which is the most common measure of differences between dinucleotide relative abundance "genomic signatures"
gold_ideal_answer: ['The concept of a genomic signature was introduced with the observation of species-type specific Dinucleotide Relative Abundance Profiles (DRAPs). The set of dinucleotide odds ratios or \'general design\' is a remarkably stable property of the DNA of an organism, and can be used to discriminate between sequences from different organisms. The average absolute dinucleotide relative abundance difference is termed delta-distance. Delta-distance is the most commonly used measure of differences bwetween "genomic signatures". Delta-distances between different genomic sequences in the same species are low, and are generally smaller than the between-species delta-distances.']
pred_ideal_answer: The documents discuss dinucleotide relative abundance as a measure of genomic signatures. Specifically, the research explores skew of mononucleotide frequencies, relative abundance of dinucleotides, and DNA strand asymmetry, all related to dinucleotide relative abundance. The analysis focuses on identifying genomic signatures based on these abundance patterns.

body: Which gene strand is targeted by transcription-coupled repair (TCR)?
gold_ideal_answer: ['Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER) removes a variety of helix-distorting lesions from DNA. It has two sub-pathways, the global genome (gg) NER and the transcription-coupled repair (TCR). TCR is triggered when a RNA polymerase, translocating along the transcribed strand, is arrested at a lesion or unusual structure in the DNA. TCR is dedicated to target and repair the transcribed strand of an active gene.']
pred_ideal_answer: Transcription-coupled DNA repair (TCR) targets the DNA strand that is actively being transcribed. This mechanism ensures accurate DNA replication and repair by focusing on regions where DNA damage has arisen due to RNA polymerase activity. The process is genomic context-dependent, meaning the specific strand targeted can vary.

body: Which hormone receptor function is altered in patients with Donohue syndrome?
gold_ideal_answer: Donohue syndrome (leprechaunism) is a rare, recessively inherited disorder of extreme insulin resistance due to mutations in the insulin receptor gene causing either defects in insulin binding or receptor autophosphorylation and tyrosine kinase activity. This syndrome is characterized by excessive hyperglycemia with hyperinsulinism, pre- and postnatal growth retardation, distinct dysmorphism and early death. Progressive obstructive cardiomyopathy and renal tubular dysfunction have been described in patients with Donohue syndrome. Milder form of insulin resistance due to insulin receptor gene mutation is coined as Rabson-Mendenhall syndrome.
pred_ideal_answer: Patients with Donohue syndrome exhibit alterations in insulin receptor function. Specifically, the severe progressive obstructive cardiomyopathy and renal tubular dysfunction observed in several cases are linked to decreased insulin receptor autophosphorylation due to a novel INSR mutation. This disruption directly impacts the normal signaling pathway initiated by insulin, ultimately contributing to the syndrome’s characteristic manifestations. The document highlights mutations in the insulin receptor gene as the core cause of this dysfunction.

body: Which are the newly identified DNA nucleases that can be used to treat thalassemia?
gold_ideal_answer: The newly identified DNA nucleases that can be used to treat thalassemia are the transcription activator-like effector nucleases (TALEN). These are engineered proteins able to stimulate targeted integration of therapeutic wild-type beta-globin cDNAs to the endogenous beta-globin locus.
pred_ideal_answer: The document describes the use of zinc-finger nucleases and TALENs for gene correction in β-thalassemia and α-thalassemia iPS cells. Zinc-finger nucleases were used to correct α-thalassemia, while TALENs were used for gene correction in β-thalassemia iPS cells. Nuclease-mediated gene editing was also described using homologous recombination.

body: Which value of nuchal translucency thickness is set as the threshold for high-risk for Down Syndrome?

body: What is the prognostic role of thyroid hormone in patients with heart failure?
gold_exact_answer: ['There is a relationship between altered thyroid profile and mortality in patients with heart failure']

pred_ideal_answer: Thyroid hormone is a critical determinant of myocardial performance in patients with heart failure, as indicated by multiple studies. Specifically, research has identified its prognostic role in various aspects of heart failure. One study focused on thyroid stimulating hormone levels in cardiomyopathy patients, while others explored the impact of sub-clinical hypothyroidism in chronic heart failure outpatients and the long-term prognostic value of triiodothyronine concentration in elderly patients with heart failure. These findings suggest thyroid hormone plays a significant role in predicting outcomes for this patient population.

body: Abnormality in which vertebral region is important in the Bertolotti's syndrome?
gold_ideal_answer: Lumbosacral vertebral region is implicated in the Bertolotti's syndrome. Lumbosacral transitional vertebra is an anatomical variation of the fifth lumbar vertebra in which an enlarged transverse process can form a joint or fusion with the sacrum or ilium. Patients often complain of intractable sciatica that arises from impingement of the nerve root extraforaminally by compression caused by the enlarged transverse process.
pred_ideal_answer: The abnormalities associated with Bertolotti’s syndrome are primarily found in the transitional vertebrae of the lumbar spine. These vertebrae exhibit a partial fusion of the articulating processes of the lower lumbar vertebrae with the sacrum, leading to a characteristic transitional vertebra. This abnormal vertebral region is a key feature of the syndrome and often contributes to symptoms such as back pain and sciatica.

body: Which gene is required for the efficient function of clopidogrel?
gold_ideal_answer: The prodrug clopidogrel requires activation by cytochrome P-450 (CYP) enzymes for its antiplatelet effect. The CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles had a gene dose effect on the pharmacodynamics and composite ischemic events of clopidogrel in our study population. body: How many genes outside of the MHC locus have been genetically associated to Rheumatoid Arthritis through GWAS?
gold_ideal_answer: Large genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified more than 30 loci involved in RA pathogenesis. To date, over 30 non-MHC RA-associated loci have been identified in humans, and over 100 arthritis-associated loci have been identified in rodent models of RA. The most relevant non-HLA gene single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) associated with RA include PTPN22, IL23R, TRAF1, CTLA4, IRF5, STAT4, CCR6, PADI4. Previous studies demonstrate that 6 of the established non-HLA CD and RA risk loci (out of 26 loci for each disease) are shared between both diseases.

body: Is the transcriptional regulator BACH1 an activator or a repressor?
gold_ideal_answer: BACH1, a basic leucine zipper mammalian transcriptional repressor, negatively regulates heme oxygenase 1 (HMOX1), a key cytoprotective enzyme that has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. In the absence of elevated intracellular heme or oxidative stress, BACH1 functions as a repressor of the enhancers of heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) gene (Hmox-1) by forming heterodimers with the small Maf proteins such as MafK. Bach1 is recruited to a subset of p53 target genes and contributes to impeding p53 action by promoting histone deacetylation.
pred_ideal_answer: BACH1 is a specific repressor of HMOX1 that is inactivated by arsenite.

body: What is the name of Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitor that can be used for treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia?
gold_ideal_answer: Ibrutinib is the covalent inhibitor of Bruton's tyrosine kinase that can be used for treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). Ibrutinib has shown highly encouraging results in phase I/II trials in patients with treatment-naive, relapsed and refractory CLL even in the presence of high risk disease or poor prognostic markers. Ibrutinib demonstrated that Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibition sensitizes CLL cells to apoptosis and alters their migratory behavior. Ibrutinib has excellent activity in other B cell malignancies, including in particular mantle cell lymphoma and Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia.
pred_ideal_answer: Ibrutinib is a Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitor used in the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia. PCI-32765 is another Bruton’s tyrosine kinase inhibitor used for lymphoid malignancies.

body: Which JAK (Janus kinase) inhibitor is approved for treatment of rheumatoid arthritis?
gold_ideal_answer:
Tofacitinib (or CP690.550) is an oral JAK (Janus kinase) inhibitor that is approved for treatment of rheumatoid arthritis. Tofacitinib inhibits JAK family kinase members, in particular JAK1 and JAK3, achieving a broad limitation of inflammation by interfering with several cytokine receptors. Tofacitinib has also a proven efficacy as an immunosuppressive regimen after renal transplantation.  
GLPG-0634 and INCB18424 are other JAK kinase inhibitors that are being studied for treatment of rheumatoid arthritis.
pred_ideal_answer: Tofacitinib

body: How can the expression of SerH3 immobilization antigen be regulated?
gold_ideal_answer: The expression of Tetrahymena surface proteins serotype H3 (SerH3) is under temperature regulation. SerH3 is expressed when cells are incubated between the temperatures of 20 and 35 degrees C.
pred_ideal_answer: The expression of SerH3 immobilization antigen can be regulated primarily by a temperature-dependent change in the stability of its mRNA. Specifically, a shift in incubation temperature rapidly affects SerH3 mRNA stability. This change can directly impact the mRNA's structure or involve regulatory factors, or it could simply affect the mRNA stability.

body: Which is the third subunit of the TSC1-TSC2 complex upstream of mTORC1?
gold_ideal_answer: TBC1D7 was identified as a stably associated and ubiquitous third core subunit of the TSC1-TSC2 complex. body: Which methyl-CpG-binding protein when mutant becomes the hallmark for Rett syndrome?
gold_ideal_answer: Rett syndrome (RTT) was shown to be caused by mutations in the methyl-CpG-binding protein 2 (MECP2) gene, with molecular studies identifying MECP2 mutations in up to 80% of classic RTT patients. MECP2 protein was found to assist in the transcriptional silencing process via DNA methylation. We therefore hypothesize that disruption of this gene alters the normal developmental expression of various other genes, some of which must account for the peculiar neurologic phenotype of RTT.
pred_ideal_answer: Mutations in the methyl-CpG-binding protein 2 gene (MECP2) are identified in the majority of females with Rett syndrome (RTT), an X-linked dominant neurodevelopmental disorder.

Species-specific differences in Xist expression and function have also been explored, highlighting the complexity of this process.

body: Describe a diet that reduces the chance of kidney stones.
gold_ideal_answer:
People can help prevent kidney stones by making changes in fluid intake and, depending on the type of kidney stone, changes in consumption of sodium, animal protein, calcium, and oxalate.
Drinking enough fluids each day is the best way to help prevent most types of kidney stones. Health care providers recommend that a person drink 2 to 3 liters of fluid a day. People with cystine stones may need to drink even more. Though water is best, other fluids may also help prevent kidney stones, such as citrus drinks.
pred_ideal_answer: Dietary recommendations, such as those including beef or plant proteins, may be one of the best ways to reduce the risk of calcium oxalate stones.  Studies have shown that dietary calcium from dairy and nondairy sources, along with diets with either beef or plant proteins, are associated with a lower risk of kidney stone formation.  The document emphasizes that diet is a key element in managing kidney stones, but it should always be considered in conjunction with present metabolic risk factors.

body: What is the methyl donor of DNA (cytosine-5)-methyltransferases?

body: Which is the causative agent of malaria?
gold_ideal_answer: ['Four Plasmodium species commonly infect humans (Plasmodium falciparum, Plasmodium vivax, Plasmodium malariae and Plasmodium ovale). Plasmodium falciparum infects about 5-10% of the world human population per year and it is the causative agent of the most severe and lethal form of malaria. P. falciparum  causes fatal cerebral malaria and is responsible for most deaths, particularly in pregnant women and children under the age of five. P. falciparum is transmitted to the human host by Anopheles mosquitoes and is the most tremendous malaria vector in sub-Saharan Africa. Plasmodium vivax is the causative agent of benign malaria in more temperate climates of the world. Plasmodium gallinaceum is the main bird malaria causative agent and  Plasmodium yoelli is the principle rodent malaria agent.']
pred_ideal_answer: Plasmodium

body: How many TAp73 isoforms have been identified in humans?
gold_ideal_answer: ["The TP73 gene, due to the presence of two promoters (P1 and P2) in its 5' flanking region, encodes a fully transcriptionally active domain (TAp73) and the amino terminus deleted (ΔNp73). TAp73 possesses pro-apoptotic properties, while deltaNp73 has anti-apoptotic functions. Alternative 3'-end splicing results in generation of at least seven TAp73 distinctive isoforms ( α, β, γ, etc ).", "The Trp73 gene belongs to the p53 family of transcription factors and, like the other members, is transcribed into different isoforms [1-4]. TP73 gene contains two promoters, encoding the transcriptional domain-containing (TAp73) and the amino deleted (DNp73) isoforms [5, 6]. Furthermore alternative splicing at the 3'-end (to generate a, b, g, etc isoforms) and 5'-end (to generate D2, D3 and D2-3 isoforms) results in generation of at least 14 different transcripts, with different abilities to promote or repress apoptosis [7, 8]. body: What is the genetic basis of Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome?
gold_exact_answer: ['Mutations or/and deletions in the genes of the cAMP-response element binding protein-BP (CREBBP) (50-60% of the cases) and of the homologous gene E1A-binding protein (EP300) at 22q13 (5%).']
gold_ideal_answer: ['Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome (RTS) is a rare autosomal dominant disorder (prevalence 1:125,000) characterised by broad thumbs and halluces, facial dysmorphism, psychomotor development delay, skeletal defects, abnormalities in the posterior fossa and short stature. The known genetic causes are a microdeletion at 16p13.3 or mutations or deletions of the cAMP-response element binding protein-BP (CREBBP) (50-60% of the cases) and of the homologous gene E1A-binding protein (EP300) at 22q13 (5%). Direct sequencing of CREBBP performed in 13 RSTS patients identified the three zinc fingers (CH1, CH2, CH3) and HAT domain as mutational hotspots. Thus about 55% of patients have cytogenetic or molecular abnormalities in the Crebbp or E1A binding protein p300 (Ep300) gene, leaving the diagnosis in 45% of patients to rest on clinical features only.']
pred_ideal_answer: Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome is primarily caused by mutations in the transcriptional co-activator CBP gene. However, variations exist, including mutations in CREBBP and atypical presentations with normal FISH results. The document highlights cases identified through CREBBP gene analysis and broader spectrum mutations observed in Indian patients.

body: Which type of lung cancer is the most strongly associated with Lambert-Eaton syndrome?
gold_ideal_answer: ['Small-cell lung cancer is most commonly associated with Lambert-Eaton syndrome. Case reports suggest that other non-small-cell lung cancer types, such as large-cell neuroendocrine carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma,  can be also very rarely associated this syndrome.']
pred_ideal_answer: Lambert-Eaton syndrome is most strongly associated with large-cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the lung. Several studies have reported associations between Lambert-Eaton syndrome and lung cancer, with large-cell neuroendocrine carcinoma being particularly prevalent. Specifically, one study noted an association with small cell lung cancer, while another reported a combined small cell and large cell lung cancer association.  Another study found that Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome has a more progressive course in patients with lung cancer.  The document highlights a strong connection between Lambert-Eaton syndrome and large-cell neuroendocrine carcinoma.

body: Which drug should be used as an antidote in benzodiazepine overdose?
gold_ideal_answer: ['Flumazenil should be used in all patients presenting with suspected benzodiazepine overdose. Flumazenil is a  potent benzodiazepine receptor antagonist that competitively blocks the central effects of benzodiazepines and reverses behavioral, neurologic, and electrophysiologic effects of benzodiazepine overdose. Clinical efficacy and safety of flumazenil in treatment of benzodiazepine overdose has been confirmed in a number of rigorous clinical trials. In addition, flumazenil is also useful to to reverse benzodiazepine induced sedation and to and to diagnose benzodiazepine overdose.']
pred_ideal_answer: Flumazenil is an antidote for benzodiazepine toxicity. The dosage of flumazenil used in this study ranged from 0.25 mg to 3 mg (average 0.87 +/- 0.74 mg).

body: Which enzyme is involved in the maintenance of DNA (cytosine-5-)-methylation?
gold_ideal_answer: ['The mammalian DNA (cytosine-5) methyltransferase 1, DNMT1 is the major enzyme responsible for the maintenance of the DNA methylation patterns on the newly synthesized strand after DNA replication. DNMT1 prefers hemimethylated DNA and during DNA replication methylates hemimethylated CpG sites by copying methylation patterns from the parental DNA strand to the newly synthesized daughter strand. The equivalent of DNMT1 in plants is MET1.']
pred_ideal_answer: Several DNA methyltransferases are involved in the maintenance of DNA (cytosine-5-)-methylation. Specifically, Dnmt3b joins the dance in maintaining DNA methylation, and DNA 5-methylcytosine demethylation activities of the mammalian DNA methyltransferases also contribute. Furthermore, cooperative activity of DNA methyltransferases is observed in Arabidopsis thaliana.

body: Which is the receptor for substrates of Chaperone Mediated Autophagy?
gold_ideal_answer: ['Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a lysosomal pathway for selective removal of damaged cytosolic proteins. The LAMP2A (Lysosome-associated membrane protein 2 isoform A) functions as a receptor for cytosolic proteins and also as essential component of the CMA translocation complex [28]. Cytosolic substrate proteins bind to monomers of LAMP-2A, which then multimerizes to form the complex required for substrate transmembrane import.']
pred_ideal_answer: The chaperone-mediated autophagy receptor organizes in dynamic protein complexes at the lysosomal membrane. This receptor is responsible for recognizing and transporting proteins destined for degradation via chaperone-mediated autophagy. Other relevant research suggests altered dynamics of the lysosomal receptor for chaperone-mediated autophagy with age, and selective degradation of annexins by chaperone-mediated autophagy relies on this same receptor.
